Não percebi o quanto era difícil até o final de minha missão, depois de dois anos. Foi quando percebi que tinha começado a sofrer de transtorno de estresse pós-traumático, graças ao trabalho dos médicos e do psicólogo. No entanto, eu havia passado por prisão, tortura física e psicológica e exílio. 
A Guatemala é um paraíso na terra, com monumentos que testemunham a beleza, a engenhosidade e os cálculos precoces dos grupos nativos. Ao mesmo tempo, havia a ferocidade de matar uns aos outros de forma cruel. Prefiro não evocar as imagens que me vêm à mente. Foi uma missão que produziu um resultado bastante razoável.
SL: Em 2003, depois que Néstor Kirchner foi eleito presidente, o senhor foi nomeado Ministro das Relações Exteriores, um cargo estratégico que ocupou por quase três anos. O que pode nos dizer sobre essa experiência? Quais foram as personalidades mundiais que mais o impressionaram durante esses anos?
RB: Sempre vivi momentos interessantes. A Argentina estava inadimplente com organizações multilaterais e detentores de títulos. Tivemos que sair dessa situação. Eu era Ministro das Relações Exteriores, Comércio Exterior e Culto. Portanto, é um mundo bastante imponente.
Para se ter uma ideia do ritmo de um Ministro das Relações Exteriores, a média durante os três anos foi de 275 dias de avião por ano. Isso é o que se faz quando se é jovem. Não sei se faria isso novamente, porque é uma questão de resistência física. Esse ritmo tem um impacto na coluna vertebral, nas vértebras, e seu humor é afetado. É como se a vida passasse mais rápido.		
Em termos de personalidades, eu conhecia todas as pessoas que estavam no comando na época. Devo dizer que a pessoa que mais me impressionou, e isso foi em uma época em que ele ainda não tinha dado tudo de si, foi Vladimir Putin. Fiquei realmente deslumbrado. Ele era um homem com poucas ferramentas à sua disposição.  A Rússia tem uma ferramenta, que é seu território, mas tem poucos habitantes, todos concentrados dos Urais para o oeste. A infraestrutura da Rússia estava esgotada e a União das Repúblicas Socialistas Soviéticas tinha acabado de ser dissolvida. A liderança de Boris Yeltsin havia sido errática e sem direção. Putin tinha uma visão extraordinária do futuro. Ele trouxe ordem e direção. Esse objetivo estava ligado a muitos elementos fundamentais da condição russa. Ele não queria uma Rússia czarista. Não rejeitava o capitalismo dogmaticamente, mas também não estava preparado para entregar seus melhores ativos à parte mais perversa do Ocidente. Portanto, ele me pareceu um líder titânico. Foi essa a impressão que tive.
SL: Que outras figuras o impressionaram?
RB: Outras pessoas também me impressionaram muito. Algumas não estavam em seu melhor momento. Por exemplo, Hosni Mubarak me recebeu no Palácio Ras el-Tin nas primeiras horas da manhã. Era possível ver que ele já havia sido um homem de enorme energia e vontade férrea, mas não tinha mais a força necessária.
Também fiquei muito impressionado com Dominique de Villepin, que era um homem extraordinário. Na época, ele era Ministro das Relações Exteriores. Era um homem extraordinário, um aventureiro, muito ousado. Ele tinha características que não eram estritamente democráticas. Ele tinha uma certa visão autocrática, principalmente na forma como queria que a França fosse vista no mundo. Mas eu me dava bem com ele. Era um relacionamento muito intenso. Eles não duram muito tempo. Podem ser confundidas com amizade, mas, na verdade, o importante é ter poder. Você é interessante na medida em que tem poder neste mundo. Se você se distanciar do poder, perderá o interesse.
Não quero me esquecer de uma pessoa que está atualmente na prisão. Estou falando de Bo Xilaï, que era o Ministro do Comércio Exterior da China na época. Ele era um homem excepcional, por causa de sua inteligência e dinamismo. Era um chinês alto e magro. Havia sido campeão olímpico de natação em seu país. Parece-me que ele tinha uma concepção mais maoista do que o partido queria. Sua desgraça está, sem dúvida, ligada a isso, porque ele poderia perfeitamente ter sido o número um. Ele era um homem que eu conhecia bem. Viajei muito pela China, nada menos que seis vezes. Ele foi à Argentina três vezes. Portanto, eu me lembro dele como um homem com quem eu gostava de conversar e discutir coisas. De fato, todas as bases do importante comércio que temos com a China, os protocolos fitossanitários, a redução das tarifas alfandegárias, foram negociados naquela época. Essa ainda é a estrutura que temos hoje.
Acho que essas são as personalidades que me impressionaram, juntamente com o Ministro das Relações Exteriores do Brasil na época, que agora é assessor do Presidente Lula, Celso Amorim. Ele também é um homem extraordinário, hiperprofissional, inteligente, criativo, enfim, tudo o que se pode dizer de uma boa pessoa.
SL: Em 2005, após três anos intensos como Ministro das Relações Exteriores, o senhor foi eleito deputado federal. O que pode nos dizer sobre esse período como representante do povo argentino?
RB: Tenho que admitir que não era para mim. Nem todo mundo é adequado para cargos públicos. A Câmara dos Deputados da República Federal da Argentina tem 253 membros. Pareceu-me que havia muita gente falando apenas para ter sua participação registrada. Foi um duelo de vaidades que achei insuportável. Não tinha nada a ver com o sofrimento do país. Portanto, não era um trabalho em que eu me sentisse confortável.
Sempre fiz meu trabalho, de forma acadêmica. Nunca perdi uma sessão. Eu ia, estudava, mas não sentia que estava contribuindo para nada. De fato, depois de perder as eleições por poucos votos na província em que nasci, Santa Fé, para um candidato chamado Hermes Binner, que já era governador, renunciei à Câmara dos Deputados porque senti que não poderia me candidatar a governador de Santa Fé e continuar representando o povo da cidade de Buenos Aires como se fosse a mesma coisa. Renunciei quando faltavam dois anos para o fim do meu mandato e fui trabalhar.
SL: Entre 2011 e 2013, o senhor foi Secretário da SEDRONAR, responsável pelo programa de prevenção à dependência de drogas e combate ao tráfico de drogas. O que pode nos dizer sobre esse assunto?
RB: Em primeiro lugar, a maior tragédia é vivida pelas pessoas mais pobres, porque são elas que usam as piores substâncias.
Em segundo lugar, os Estados Unidos são os maiores consumidores. O problema não poderá ser resolvido enquanto tivermos uma demanda tão forte quanto a dos Estados Unidos. Essas são as mesmas pessoas que querem nos vender a receita para resolver o problema. Nossos países, do México à Argentina, não têm as ferramentas institucionais para combater esse comércio. 
Por isso, os Estados Unidos estão pressionando o Congresso, fazendo lobby para nos impor suas ideias, como a famosa “guerra às drogas”, que foi a pior maneira de lidar com o problema. Como a América Latina não está unida, torna-se muito difícil coordenar uma agenda única.
SL: Entre 2013 e 2017, o senhor foi presidente da Aeropuertos de Argentina 2000, empresa que administra a concessão de cerca de trinta aeroportos no país. Conte-nos um pouco sobre essa experiência.
RB: Foi uma ótima experiência porque foi muito estimulante do ponto de vista tecnológico e técnico. O mundo da aviação e dos aeroportos é muito complexo e está em constante mudança. Até a crise da Covid-19, era necessário cada vez mais espaço porque havia cada vez mais voos. Como a empresa tinha aeroportos em outras partes do mundo, na Itália, em Portugal, no Brasil e no Peru, isso me permitiu fazer muitos intercâmbios e me manter a par das últimas tecnologias, que muitas vezes vinham do setor militar.
Foi uma experiência muito agradável que terminou mal porque o presidente Macri pediu ao proprietário da empresa para me demitir. É preciso dizer que nunca fui uma pessoa dócil. Falei o que pensava com o Ministro de Infraestrutura, e isso nem sempre é bem recebido. Por isso, ele pediu minha demissão. Então, o proprietário da empresa, que é um homem muito interessante, me promoveu a presidente da empresa, que é a empresa controladora. Tínhamos a empresa de obras públicas, a empresa de aeroportos, a empresa de fabricação de microcondutores, tínhamos terras. Era uma grande diversidade de atividades e também muito agradável.
Mas quando o presidente Fernandez me convidou para ser embaixador no Chile, não hesitei nem por um momento. Seria um processo fantástico em um país com uma idiossincrasia muito interessante. Como diria Neruda, “admito que vivi”.
SL: O senhor foi embaixador da República Argentina no Chile durante quatro anos. O que pode nos dizer sobre esse cargo e a situação das relações entre os dois países?
RB: Vamos começar com a questão mais complexa. Há uma tensão sobre o território. O Chile é um país que sempre tem o território em mente e, sem dúvida, interpreta excessivamente as atitudes da Argentina, que são muito mais produto de negligência do que de vontade.
A Argentina tem muitos problemas, mas há um que ela não tem: o chauvinismo. Adoramos os estrangeiros e sempre os recebemos de braços abertos. Nunca tivemos problemas com os países vizinhos. O mesmo não acontece no Chile, que nos atribui projetos que a Argentina nunca teve, como a existência de um plano há 120 anos para assumir o controle de parte do Chile. A Argentina não pode apoiar um plano por 120 dias. Imagine 120 anos.  
Por outro lado, nossas relações comerciais bilaterais com o Chile são as maiores do mundo. A Argentina tem um superávit comercial maior com o Chile do que com qualquer outro país. Portanto, essas são duas economias complementares. As culturas também são complementares. O esforço que precisamos fazer é tentar entender um ao outro para que não cometamos erros em nossas interpretações políticas. Esse é o papel do embaixador.
SL: Como foi a comemoração do 50º aniversário do golpe de Estado de Pinochet no Chile?
RB: Houve uma coisa que realmente me impressionou. A comemoração mostrou que 40 a 50% da população chilena ainda é muito apegada a algumas das coisas que Pinochet defendia, ou seja, ordem, autoridade, respeito pelas hierarquias e uma obsessão por manter o controle de tudo. No Chile, tudo é medido: o número de horas trabalhadas, a quantidade de açúcar mascavo consumida. É um hábito extraordinário que a Argentina não tem, mas, ao mesmo tempo, é um pouco rígido.
Como resultado, metade dos chilenos não participou das comemorações. Elas foram muito emocionantes e muito ricas do ponto de vista histórico. Foi um privilégio estar lá. Mas, para ser sincero, devo dizer que elas estavam longe de ser unânimes.
SL: Você é um escritor prolífico e, como todos os escritores, é acima de tudo um grande leitor. Quais obras tiveram o maior impacto sobre você?
RB: São muitas, porque é verdade que sou um grande leitor. Atualmente, estou lendo vários livros ao mesmo tempo: o último livro de Ariel Dorfman, um livro da trilogia de Primo Levi e um livro de Carlos Droguet sobre a morte de Salvador Allende. 
Se eu tivesse que citar três obras, escolheria primeiro Em Busca do Tempo Perdido, de Marcel Proust. Em momentos de grande angústia, devo dizer que ler Proust me acalmava muito, mesmo que a história não fosse feliz. Mas essa genialidade, essa capacidade de observação, essa sensibilidade aguçada me trouxeram uma grande paz interior. 
O Quarteto de Alexandria, de Lawrence Durrell, é uma obra que me influenciou de várias maneiras, especialmente os três primeiros volumes, e um pouco menos o último, Clea. Mas Justine, Mountolive e Baltazar são livros que sempre releio. 
Por fim, a obra de James Joyce, incluindo Finnegans Wake, que é um livro experimental, muito difícil de entender, traduzir ou transformar em uma peça.
Em meio a tudo isso, há muitos escritores que me impressionaram. Eu poderia mencionar Emmanuel Carrère, Edouard Limonov e toda a nova literatura sul-americana, que está repleta de autores.
Eu realmente gosto de bons escritores. Você pode encontrar um bom escritor em alguém que não é escritor. Recentemente, um amigo meu foi à Palestina, um amigo que é prefeito da Recoleta, um município da metrópole de Santiago, Daniel Jadue, e ele escreveu um livro sobre essa viagem. Ele foi visitar seus parentes e honrar os túmulos de seus antepassados.  Ele escreveu sobre o terror de ter sua entrada recusada, os problemas burocráticos e os maus-tratos que poderia sofrer. É um ótimo livro. No entanto, ele não é um escritor. Ele é um político do Partido Comunista. Mas é um livro magnífico. Não tenho preconceitos quando se trata de escolher o que ler agora ou no futuro.
SL: Você também é um escritor prolífico. Seu último livro chama-se Lawfare e é um grande sucesso comercial. O que pode nos dizer sobre ele?
RB: É um livro que evoca os métodos opressivos dos Estados Unidos, que tem sido uma potência hegemônica e agora está tentando preservar sua posição dominante no concerto das nações. Os métodos de dominação são muitos: há o soft power e o hard power. Com poucas exceções, as forças armadas são altamente desacreditadas em todos os nossos países. 
Como resultado, o judiciário encontrou um terreno fértil. Começamos com a perseguição pelos tribunais e pela mídia de figuras políticas que colocam em risco os grandes lucros do capital. O objetivo é continuar a loucura da fase atual do capitalismo, que tem um componente financeiro muito forte e o monopólio da produção de tecnologia. 
A concentração de renda nunca foi tão alta como é hoje, com o fenômeno dos bilionários. É um fenômeno que precisa ser estudado, pois nos leva a achar naturais certas coisas que não são. As palavras são esvaziadas de seu significado real e usadas com um significado diferente. Assim, somos privados dos elementos que nos permitem construir nosso pensamento e conceber atos de rebelião e resistência. Além disso, o sistema atual é insustentável porque está destruindo o meio ambiente. 
SL: Além de escritor, você também é poeta. Você escreveu várias obras, como Espejo negro e Esplendor. Qual é o papel da poesia?
RB: Essa é uma boa pergunta, porque é uma pergunta que um verdadeiro poeta nunca faria a si mesmo. Na verdade, escrevemos apesar de nós mesmos. Há um verso de Caetano Veloso em uma música que diz: “Só canto o que não posso calar”. Isso é poesia. As palavras se juntam a despeito do poeta. Muitas vezes, o poeta escreve um verso e ignora o impacto de suas palavras na alma de uma pessoa sensível.
Por muitos anos, os poetas foram augúrios sociais que ficavam entre a lira terrena e a lira celestial. Hoje, esse não é mais o caso, porque as pessoas não leem mais muita poesia e porque os poetas não têm mais o mesmo prestígio. Por exemplo, um fenômeno como Yves Bonnefoy na França não se repetiu na poesia. Mas acho que a poesia sempre existirá e a voz do poeta será importante sempre que houver retrocessos civilizacionais.
SL: Vamos falar sobre futebol. Você é irmão de Marcelo Bielsa, o técnico mundialmente famoso. Mas você é, acima de tudo, um fã do Newell's Old Boys e até escreveu um livro sobre o seu amado clube. O que o futebol significa para você?
RB: Não tenho uma única lembrança em minha vida, boa ou ruim, que não possa associar a uma partida de futebol do Newell's, a um time ou a um jogador que eu adorava na época. Desde os quatro ou cinco anos de idade, todas as minhas lembranças estão associadas a um episódio de futebol. Portanto, eu diria que o futebol é uma segunda natureza para mim. Com o passar dos anos, fui me tornando cada vez mais seletivo. Quando Marcelo estava no comando do Leeds United, eu era torcedor do Newell's e do Leeds. Agora, naturalmente, acompanho a seleção uruguaia e espero que ela tenha o maior sucesso possível.
É verdade que em um determinado momento da minha vida eu realmente acompanhava futebol. Nunca perdi um jogo da primeira, segunda ou terceira divisão. Infelizmente, a forte presença do dinheiro no futebol o tornou menos atraente. O esporte era muito mais atraente quando estava próximo do mundo amador. Com o profissionalismo, há menos espaço para sentimentos. Os jogadores mudam de clube depois de seis meses e é difícil se apegar a um jogador específico. Na minha opinião, perdemos gradualmente aquela humanidade que tornava o futebol tão cativante. 
SL: O que Diego Maradona simboliza para o povo argentino?
RB: Diego Maradona é tanto o homem que todos nós gostaríamos de ser quanto o homem que somos, sem nos atrevermos a admitir isso. Gostamos muito do Messi. Mas idolatramos Diego. Diego nos emociona. Messi nos encanta. São sentimentos diferentes.
Nós nos identificamos com a vida que o pobre Diego teve, com todos os seus excessos. Admiramos sua coragem em ignorar as autoridades oficiais, seus compromissos donquichottescos que duraram toda a sua vida.
Ele expressa perfeitamente a verdadeira essência da Argentina: indivíduos capazes de ser gênios, mas também de roubar um garfo em um restaurante. Diego era isso. Ficamos sem palavras em seus momentos de genialidade e, em seus momentos de fraqueza, rimos baixinho, sem demonstrar muito, mas como se disséssemos: “Ah, esse Diego”. Ele é alguém que amamos.
SL: Uma pergunta sobre a América Latina. Como você vê o continente hoje? Quais são seus principais desafios?
RB: É muito difícil falar sobre o continente porque esse é um dos períodos mais fragmentados desde a década de 1980. Não há continuidade no que diz respeito a governos progressistas. Sem união, não é possível atingir a massa necessária para poder enfrentar certas questões em organizações multilaterais com uma posição única. Portanto, parece-me que cada país tem sua própria agenda.
Onde você coloca o Peru, um país que está passando por uma enorme crise institucional há dez anos? Onde você coloca a Colômbia, que depois de muitos anos de hegemonia de direita agora é liderada por um governo de esquerda? Os processos sociais levam mais tempo do que os períodos de governo.
Estamos buscando uma liderança providencial, enquanto o coletivo me parece ser a única maneira de encontrar uma solução para os problemas do continente. Portanto, é muito difícil falar de uma América Latina.
SL: Como você vê o fortalecimento dos BRICS e o surgimento de um novo mundo multipolar?
RB: Antes de mais nada, gostaria de enfatizar que a existência do BRICS é positiva. Mas vou criticar alguns aspectos. Qual é o problema? A história dos BRICS mostrou que a fraca institucionalização é um obstáculo quando os ventos estão contra nós. Os BRICS desfrutaram de um período de expansão que coincidiu com o aumento do valor das matérias-primas. Após a crise de 2008, houve uma calmaria e os BRICS quase não foram mais mencionados. 
Hoje, eles estão voltando graças à energia de Lula, que é como um peixe na água nesse universo. O Brasil tem sorte de ter Lula, porque Lula é como um Mandela brasileiro. Portanto, ele é uma figura de proa maravilhosa. Como o Brasil tem excelentes executivos de política externa, isso parece funcionar. Mas sempre é preciso observar a estrutura institucional. Se ela for sólida, os BRICS têm uma chance de perdurar. Por outro lado, se for fraca, eles ficarão vulneráveis. No momento, os BRICS são mais uma boa ideia do que uma ferramenta poderosa. 
A multipolaridade é a tendência imediata, mas não é a tendência definitiva. Eu gostaria que fosse. Os Estados Unidos continuarão sendo importantes e líderes em determinadas áreas por alguns anos. 
SL: O senhor poderia falar um pouco sobre a China?
RB: O avanço da China é mais sólido do que o recuo dos Estados Unidos. Ninguém parece ter notado que a China, que vende tudo o que se possa imaginar, não exporta ideologia, ao contrário da esquerda das décadas de 1960 e 1970, que exportava revolução. A China está exportando seu dinheiro, seus projetos, sua capacidade de realizar trabalhos com o melhor financiamento possível, a taxas que desafiam toda a concorrência. Do meu ponto de vista, essa é uma fórmula melhor. A China é inteligente o suficiente para não entrar em uma guerra. 
SL: Última pergunta, você é um grande conhecedor da França. O que a França significa para você?
RB: Se o futebol é a segunda natureza, a França é a terceira natureza, porque minha mãe e minha avó materna falavam piemontês, que é um idioma próximo ao francês. Comecei a estudar francês quando era muito jovem. De Monsieur et Madame Vincent a La condition humaine, nada do que se fala em francês é estranho para mim. É um país extraordinariamente importante porque, mais uma vez, é difícil para mim encontrar episódios em minha vida com os quais não associe um livro em francês ou o idioma francês. A França faz parte da minha vida.
en_title: Conversations with Rafael Bielsa
en_content:
Born in 1953 in Rosario, Argentina, in a family of eminent lawyers, Rafael Bielsa developed from an early age a sensitivity to the situation of the less favored, especially through reading and religion. Practising Catholic, he positioned himself against the dictatorship of the generals, which broke the constitutional order in 1976. Arrested by the military regime, he was imprisoned and tortured, sharing the fate of thousands of opponents.
With the return of democracy in 1983, Rafael Bielsa returned from forced exile in Spain, practiced the profession of lawyer and entered politics, having a brilliant career. He was Minister of Foreign Affairs in the government of Néstor Kirchner, member of Parliament and ambassador in Chile. He was also president of Aeropuertos Argentina 2000, one of the largest companies in the country.
Rafael Bielsa is also a prolific writer, author of several books, including the best-seller Lawfare. Observer of international relations, he has an experienced look at Argentina, Latin America and the emergence of a multipolar world.
In these conversations, Rafael Bielsa recalls her happy childhood in the province of Córdoba, her passion for literature and her political commitment to the least favored. He recalls the painful period of political repression, prison, torture and ostracism. He also reports his return to the country, where he held the highest positions at the service of the nation. He talks about his meetings with world leaders, including Vladimir Putin, Hosni Mubarak, Bo Xilaï and Dominique de Villepin. Passionate about football, he talks about his favorite club, the Newell's Old Boys, and about the figure of Diego Maradona.
Salim Lamrani: Rafael Bielsa, you were born in Rosario, Argentina, in a prominent family. His father was an eminent lawyer and his grandfather, a top-line jurist. What memories do you have from your childhood?
Rafael Bielsa: I have good memories of my childhood. I remember with affection from school years. I loved my grandfather's library, which was huge, with 30,000 or 40,000 volumes. I think that's where I found the pleasure of reading.
In the summer, I used to go to my mother's home village, Morteros, in the province of Córdoba, of Mediterranean style, a place that is very sweet to me. It was a very happy period that lasted until the age of 12, until high school.
SL: What were his main sources of inspiration when he was young?
RB: I read a lot. There was a collection in Argentina called “Robin Hood”, a series of adventure books by authors such as Emilio Salgari, Júlio Verne and others. My head was full of all these epics, the Tiger of Mompracem, Borneo, Java. I think my models always came from these texts, which I devour incessantly.
There was a moment we feared when children: it was the time of the nap. We didn't like her, but we had to respect her. I remember taking a book, going to the shadow and reading for hours.
In fact, there is a version of Dom Quixote illustrated by Gustave Doré, with a drawing of it with a pile of books and smoke coming out of his head, with representations of Dulcineia, the villains and so on. I have to admit that I was a little like this when I was a kid.
Between the world of opulence and the world of “workers” - as we say in Argentina - I have always had a fall for the latter. That's how my responsibility and my way of being were forged.
SL: At the age of 18, you joined the Peronist University Youth. When did you start asking political questions? How would you define yourself politically?
RB: My political questioning came through religion, because I was Roman Catholic. In the 1960s, there was a great vocation to help the needy. The fact that constantly seeing the least favored and their living conditions approached me with disruptive political methods at a time when violence was legitimized. A common phrase at the time was: “The violence from above generates the violence from below.” I think that's still true today.
So when I was 15, I started reading post-war texts, especially from Albert Camus. Some readings led me to my political position, which I adopted very early: I am a peronist. In Argentina, we speak of “national and popular”. National” implies not being servile and not copying foreign models, while ‘Popular’ refers to collective rather than individualism. In other countries, such as Chile, we speak of “Progressivism”.
I never gave it up. Unlike what usually happens, over the years I became more radical. Time, far from tempering my ideological convictions, strengthened my loyalty to my ideas, which would probably not be viable recipes today. But I remain faithful to my ideas, as Silvio Rodríguez’s song “El necio” says: “Morro como vivi”.
SL: What popular movements were a source of inspiration for the young people of their generation?
RB: Chile from 1967 to the 1973 coup, with its poets, writers, essayists, artists and musicians, had a great influence on our formation. We did not distinguish between Argentine and Chilean music, nor between Argentine and Chilean literature.
The only distinction we made was class. In Buenos Aires, there were two places: Florida, where the wealthy writers met, and Boedo, where those who were most involved with social reality were gathered. Therefore, we differed between the literature of the “Ivory Tower”, as we called it, and the proletarian literature.
I will make a confession: I was a “double agent”. In fact, there were writers of the “Torre de ivory” that I loved. I couldn't say that openly, but I was fascinated by Borges. Some poets, such as Philip James Bailey or Carlos Mastronardi, were delightful. They belonged to the “Torre de Ivory”, but I read them anyway.
SL: What led you to study law and become a lawyer? Was it a family tradition? An interest in the area?
RB: I was studying right and music at the same time. I studied music in a public conservatory and orchestra regency, but it wasn't a talented musician. I was striving and diligent, but I had no talent.
So I chose to study law. It is worth noting that studying law and becoming a lawyer is something very simple. You don't have to be too smart. Just read quickly and have a good memory.
SL: You started working at the Federal Court of Rosario in 1974, at the age of 20. Two years later, in 1976, the generals gave a military coup that shook the constitutional order. Like many young people, you rejected the coup and resisted the repressive government. You were arrested and tortured by the army in El Castillo prison. Could you tell us a little about this episode of your life?
RB: Allow me to make some corrections, or rather some clarification. The place where I got stuck was actually La Calamita. For many years, after the return of democracy, it was believed that there was only one detention center in Rosario. In fact, there were several, five in total.
During the trial, I took a look and saw that there were some things that were not right. For example, the Funes hospitalization center had no basement. El Castillo had a very narrow staircase. When I was arrested, they put a toilet in my head. So I did the reconnaissance of the place, trusting in my senses, in the touch, until I reached La Calamita. At that moment, there was no doubt in my mind. That was my place of detention.
In fact, there were two procedures in the first days of the coup. The first was the brutal, destructive and barbarous detention, but you ended up in prison in a bad way. Then there was another practice called “breaking a subversive”, which was totally illegal. These were paramilitaries who worked without any respect for the rules. It was a parallel structure. I handled this parallel structure. Fisherton, La Calamita, El Castillo and Funes were all clandestine centers. There were no official detention centers. There were people trapped with me, who I could recognize by their voices. I think two girls survived. Most of the others were murdered. This is the story I tell in the novel Operation Mexico (Operation Mexico), which was later transformed into a movie.
SL: After leaving prison, you had to exile in Spain. How did you feel when you were condemned away from your home country?
RB: It was very difficult. That's when I understood what ostracism was. Getting out and not getting back was intolerable to me. I used to go to the ports of the cities where I lived to see the boats go, wondering they were going to Argentina. That was my alienation level, my psychic collapse at the time.
This also coincided with a process called “the second counteroffensive”, in which the companions were fighting in Argentina. In fact, it was a trap created to eliminate opponents. They went through the city where I lived, Barcelona. A week later, we found out they were missing. So it was a very dark period. I did not take advantage of it except to harden my character.
Chilean exiles were very scholars. We, on the other hand, were still involved in politics in exile, which was somewhat useless. We used to meet in bars to talk about our distant homeland, a little like the Spanish Republicans who had taken refuge in France. It was an extraordinarily bitter period.
When I had the opportunity to go home, I took it immediately. A disciple of my grandfather, Roberto Luqui, whom I owe a lot, had a small role in the government. He told me: “Go home, I will protect you.” I went home, but I couldn't go back to my town. I stayed in Buenos Aires in a very precarious situation.
SL: You came back in 1980. How were their living conditions during that period until 1983 and the return of democracy?
RB: Life conditions were very difficult. I rented a very small apartment in the center of Buenos Aires, a dark and lightless apartment. I was very careful when I left. It was a semi-Clandestine life. I don't have a single pleasant memory of that time. It was a very melancholic time. You had to look for people, try to see them again.
I wanted to continue my studies, but I couldn't study at the university. I had to take separate courses. So I studied linguistics and psychoanalysis. My friend Roberto Luqui also offered me a small job to survive.
The repression had diminished a little. There were few bloody episodes after 1981, with the notable exception of the murder of two Montoneros leaders, kidnapped in Rosario. Then came the war of the Falklands. So it was a very turbulent period, of which I remember very sadly.
SL: In 1983, democracy returned. You have held several positions in the Ministry of Education and the Presidency. Could you tell us a little about these positions?
RB: I have worked in a discipline very different from that in which we work today, that is, legal computing. The computers were just beginning to be applied to law and were very rudimentary. We thought we had to learn how to program. As I had no skills in mathematics or engineering, I was studying various computer languages such as Basic, Fortran or Cobol. Computers were useful for storing large amounts of legislation. We had a universe of 23,000 laws. You had to read these laws, give them a description and store them. Then it was possible to find them quite efficiently thanks to a large computing center.
SL: Tell us about the period that passed in Italy and the functions it performed when returning.
RB: In 1985 and 1986, I moved to Italy to study. It was there that I understood the difference between leaving the country by obligation and leaving the country with the possibility of returning at any time, because it was a completely different experience. I really liked those two years. I learned a lot. It was a much nicer life. I fell in love with Rome. I think it's the place in the world I like most.
When I returned, I was appointed undersecretary of Legislative Affairs until Carlos Menem's arrival in power. The “menemism” was a peronist experiment, but it was based on Washington’s consensus in the liberalism of Milton Friedman’s Chicago School. It was a disaster because a parity system was established between the Argentine peso and the dollar. It was a fictitious parity, because the weight could not match the dollar, and was financed by debts. The debt was paid with the liquidation of public companies, at an irrisory price. It was an era, a decade, of corruption. Above all, it was a cultural break in the Republic of Argentina. It was used to say that certain types of behavior were unacceptable and that the value of things went beyond the material, monetary or financial aspect.
This decade was marked by many battles. I was very involved with the law. I wrote and published a lot. It was my most fertile period in terms of legal research.
SL: In 1995, you were a United Nations human rights expert in Guatemala. What can you tell us about this mission?
RB: Guatemala was leaving several decades of internal war, with an impressive number of deaths. The social fabric had been practically destroyed. There are 23 languages spoken in Guatemala, including Spanish. When the vote is organized, the polls sometimes need to be placed in one place and in another electoral section 300 meters away, because of rival ethnic groups. These rivalries are 500 or 600 years old and it was very difficult to operate in this context.
We denounce Otto Pérez Molina, who later became President of the Republic for serious human rights violations. During that decade, Guatemala was a society with an almost feudal right. This is how I would characterize it, based on the way it treated small people, the way it was expressed and the attributes it displayed with pride. These attributes had nothing to do with the Guatemalan tradition, but imitated the American lifestyle.
It was very common to witness the opening of clandestine graves or visit prisons. So it was a very difficult job. I didn't realize how hard it was until the end of my mission after two years. It was when I realized that I had begun to suffer from post-traumatic stress disorder, thanks to the work of doctors and the psychologist. However, I had gone through prison, physical and psychological torture and exile.
Guatemala is a paradise on earth, with monuments that witness the beauty, ingenuity and early calculations of the native groups. At the same time, there was the ferocity of killing each other cruelly. I prefer not to evoke the images that come to mind. It was a mission that produced a fairly reasonable result.
SL: In 2003, after Néstor Kirchner was elected president, you were appointed Minister of Foreign Affairs, a strategic position he held for almost three years. What can you tell us about this experience? What were the world's personalities that most impressed him during these years?
RB: I've always lived interesting moments. Argentina was inadimplent with multilateral organizations and title holders. We had to get out of this situation. I was Minister of Foreign Affairs, Foreign Trade and Cult. So it's a very imposing world.
To have an idea of the rhythm of a Minister of Foreign Affairs, the average during the three years was 275 days of plane a year. That's what you do when you're young. I don't know if I'd do that again, because it's a matter of physical resistance. This rhythm has an impact on the spine, the vertebrae, and its mood is affected. It's like life went faster.
In terms of personalities, I knew all the people who were in charge at the time. I must say that the person who impressed me most, and that was at a time when he had not yet given all of himself, was Vladimir Putin. I was really dazzled. He was a man with few tools at his disposal. Russia has a tool, which is its territory, but has few inhabitants, all concentrated from the Urals to the west. Russia's infrastructure was exhausted and the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics had just been dissolved. Boris Yeltsin's leadership had been erratic and directionless. Putin had an extraordinary vision of the future. He brought order and direction. This goal was linked to many fundamental elements of the Russian condition. He didn't want a tsarist Russia. He did not reject capitalism dogmatically, but was also not prepared to deliver its best assets to the most perverse part of the West. So he seemed like a titanic leader. That was the impression I had.
SL: What other figures impressed you?
RB: Other people impressed me too. Some were not at their best time. For example, Hosni Mubarak received me at the Ras el-Tin Palace in the early morning hours. It was possible to see that he had already been a man of enormous energy and iron will, but had no more the necessary strength.
I was also very impressed by Dominique de Villepin, who was an extraordinary man. At the time, he was Minister of Foreign Affairs. He was an extraordinary man, an adventurous, very bold. He had characteristics that were not strictly democratic. He had a certain autocratic vision, especially in the way he wanted France to be seen in the world. But I was good with him. It was a very intense relationship. They don't last long. They can be confused with friendship, but in fact, the important thing is to have power. You are interesting as you have power in this world. If you distance yourself from power, you will lose interest.
I don't want to forget someone who's currently in prison. I'm talking about Bo Xilaï, who was China's Foreign Trade Minister at the time. He was an exceptional man, because of his intelligence and dynamism. He was a tall, slim Chinese. He had been Olympic swimming champion in his country. It seems to me that he had a more Maoist conception than the party wanted. His disgrace is undoubtedly linked to that, because he could perfectly have been the number one. He was a man I knew well. I traveled a lot through China, nothing less than six times. He went to Argentina three times. So I remember him as a man I liked to talk to and discuss things with. In fact, all the bases of the important trade we have with China, the phytosanitary protocols, the reduction of customs tariffs, were negotiated at that time. This is still the structure we have today.
I think these are the personalities that impressed me, along with the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Brazil at the time, who is now an advisor to President Lula, Celso Amorim. He is also an extraordinary, hyperprofessional, intelligent, creative man, in short, all that can be said of a good person.
SL: In 2005, after three intense years as Minister of Foreign Affairs, you were elected a federal deputy. What can you tell us about this period as a representative of the Argentine people?
RB: I have to admit it wasn't for me. Not everyone is suitable for public office. The Chamber of Deputies of the Federal Republic of Argentina has 253 members. It seemed to me that there were many people talking only to have their participation recorded. It was a vanity duel I found unbearable. It had nothing to do with the country's suffering. So it wasn't a job I felt comfortable with.
I've always done my job academically. I never missed a session. I was going to study, but I didn't feel like I was contributing to anything. In fact, after losing the elections by a few votes in the province where I was born, Santa Fé, for a candidate named Hermes Binner, who was already governor, I resigned to the Chamber of Deputies because I felt that I could not apply for governor of Santa Fé and continue to represent the people of the city of Buenos Aires as if it were the same thing. I resigned when two years were missing for the end of my term and went to work.
SL: Between 2011 and 2013, you were Secretary of SEDRONAR, responsible for the drug addiction prevention program and drug trafficking. What can you tell us about this?
RB: First of all, the greatest tragedy is lived by the poorest people, because they are the ones who use the worst substances.
Secondly, the United States is the biggest consumers. The problem cannot be solved as long as we have a strong demand as that of the United States. These are the same people who want to sell us the recipe to solve the problem. Our countries, from Mexico to Argentina, do not have the institutional tools to combat this trade.
So the United States is pushing Congress, lobbying us to impose their ideas, like the famous “war on drugs”, which was the worst way to deal with the problem. Because Latin America is not united, it becomes very difficult to coordinate a single agenda.
SL: Between 2013 and 2017, you were president of Aeropuertos de Argentina 2000, which runs the concession of about thirty airports in the country. Tell us a little about this experience.
RB: It was a great experience because it was very stimulating from the technological and technical point of view. The world of aviation and airports is very complex and is constantly changing. Until the Covid-19 crisis, more and more space was needed because there were more and more flights. As the company had airports in other parts of the world, in Italy, Portugal, Brazil and Peru, this allowed me to make many exchanges and keep me aware of the latest technologies, which often came from the military sector.
It was a very pleasant experience that ended badly because President Macri asked the owner of the company to fire me. You have to say I've never been a docile person. I spoke with the Minister of Infrastructure, and that is not always well received. So he asked for my resignation. So, the owner of the company, who is a very interesting man, promoted me the president of the company, which is the controlling company. We had the public works company, the airport company, the microconductor manufacturing company, we had land. It was a great diversity of activities and also very pleasant.
But when President Fernandez invited me to be ambassador to Chile, I did not hesitate for a moment. It would be a fantastic process in a country with a very interesting idiosyncrasy. As Neruda would say, “I have lived.”
SL: You were ambassador of the Republic of Argentina in Chile for four years. What can you tell us about this position and the situation of relations between the two countries?
RB: Let's start with the most complex issue. There's a tension over the territory. Chile is a country that always has the territory in mind and, without a doubt, interprets the attitudes of Argentina, which are much more product of neglect than will.
Argentina has many problems, but there is one she does not have: chauvinism. We love foreigners and always welcome them with open arms. We've never had a problem with neighboring countries. The same does not happen in Chile, which attributes us projects that Argentina has never had, as the existence of a plan 120 years ago to take control of part of Chile. Argentina cannot support a plan for 120 days. Imagine 120 years.
On the other hand, our bilateral trade relations with Chile are the largest in the world. Argentina has a larger trading surplus with Chile than with any other country. So these are two complementary economies. Cultures are also complementary. The effort we need to do is try to understand each other so that we do not make mistakes in our political interpretations. That's the ambassador's role.
SL: How was the celebration of the 50th anniversary of the Pinochet coup in Chile?
RB: There was one thing that really impressed me. The celebration showed that 40 to 50% of the Chilean population is still very attached to some of the things Pinochet advocated, namely, order, authority, respect for hierarchies and an obsession for keeping track of everything. In Chile everything is measured: the number of hours worked, the quantity of brown sugar consumed. It is an extraordinary habit that Argentina does not have, but at the same time, it is a bit rigid.
As a result, half of the Chileans did not participate in the celebrations. They were very exciting and very rich from the historical point of view. It was a privilege to be there. But to be honest, I must say that they were far from being unanimous.
SL: You are a prolific writer and, like all writers, you are above all a great reader. What works have had the greatest impact on you?
RB: There are many, because it is true that I am a great reader. I am currently reading several books at the same time: the last book of Ariel Dorfman, a book of the trilogy of Primo Levi and a book by Carlos Droguet on the death of Salvador Allende.
If I had to quote three works, I would choose first Searching for Lost Time by Marcel Proust. In moments of great anguish, I must say that reading Proust sootheed me very much, even if the story was not happy. But this greatness, this ability to observe, this acute sensitivity brought me great inner peace.
The Quartet of Alexandria, by Lawrence Durrell, is a work that influenced me in various ways, especially the first three volumes, and a little less the last, Clea. But Justine, Mountolive and Baltazar are books that I always reread.
Finally, James Joyce's work, including Finnegans Wake, is an experimental book, very difficult to understand, translate or transform into a play.
In the midst of all this, there are many writers who impressed me. I could mention Emmanuel Carrère, Edouard Limonov and all the new South American literature, which is full of authors.
I really like good writers. You can find a good writer in someone who is not a writer. Recently, a friend of mine went to Palestine, a friend who is mayor of Recoleta, a municipality in the metropolis of Santiago, Daniel Jadue, and he wrote a book about this trip. He went to visit his relatives and honor the tombs of his ancestors. He wrote about the terror of having his entrance refused, the bureaucratic problems and the ill-treatment he could suffer. It's a great book. However, he is not a writer. He is a Communist Party politician. But it's a magnificent book. I have no prejudice when it comes to choosing what to read now or in the future.
SL: You're also a prolific writer. His last book is called Lawfare and is a great commercial success. What can you tell us about him?
RB: It is a book that evokes the oppressive methods of the United States, which has been a hegemonic power and is now trying to preserve its dominant position in the concert of nations. Domination methods are many: there is soft power and hard power. With few exceptions, the armed forces are highly discredited in all our countries.
As a result, the judiciary found a fertile ground. We begin with the persecution by the courts and the media of political figures that put at risk the big profits of the capital. The goal is to continue the madness of the current phase of capitalism, which has a very strong financial component and the monopoly of technology production.
The concentration of income has never been as high as it is today, with the phenomenon of billionaires. It is a phenomenon that needs to be studied, because it leads us to find certain things that are not natural. Words are emptied from their real meaning and used with a different meaning. Thus, we are deprived of the elements that allow us to build our thought and conceive acts of rebellion and resistance. In addition, the current system is unsustainable because it is destroying the environment.
SL: Besides, you're also a poet. You wrote several works, such as Black Espejo and Esplendor. What is the role of poetry?
RB: That is a good question, because it is a question that a true poet would never ask himself. In fact, we write despite ourselves. There is a verse of Caetano Veloso in a song that says: “I only sing what I cannot shut up.” That's poetry. The words come together despite the poet. Often, the poet writes a verse and ignores the impact of his words on the soul of a sensitive person.
For many years, the poets were social augurians who stood between the earthly lira and the heavenly lira. Today, this is no longer the case, because people no longer read much poetry and because poets no longer have the same prestige. For example, a phenomenon like Yves Bonnefoy in France did not repeat itself in poetry. But I think poetry will always exist and the voice of the poet will be important whenever there are civilisational recesses.
SL: Let's talk about football. You are the brother of Marcelo Bielsa, the world famous technician. But you are, above all, a fan of the Newell's Old Boys and even wrote a book about your beloved club. What does football mean to you?
RB: I don't have a single memory in my life, good or bad, that I can't associate with a Newell's football match, a team or a player I loved at the time. Since I was four or five years old, all my memories are associated with a football episode. So I would say that football is a second nature to me. Over the years, I became more and more selective. When Marcelo was in charge of Leeds United, I was a fan of Newell's and Leeds. Now, of course, I follow the Uruguayan selection and hope it has the greatest possible success.
It is true that at a certain time of my life I really accompanied football. I never lost a first, second or third division game. Unfortunately, the strong presence of money in football made him less attractive. The sport was much more attractive when it was close to the amateur world. With professionalism, there is less room for feelings. Players change club after six months and it is difficult to cling to a specific player. In my opinion, we gradually lost that humanity that made football so captivating.
SL: What does Diego Maradona symbolize for the Argentine people?
RB: Diego Maradona is so much the man we all would like to be as the man we are, without daring to admit it. We really like Messi. But we idolize Diego. Diego thrills us. Messi delights us. They're different feelings.
We identified ourselves with the life that poor Diego had, with all his excesses. We admire your courage to ignore the official authorities, your donquichottesco commitments that have lasted your entire life.
It perfectly expresses the true essence of Argentina: individuals capable of being geniuses, but also of stealing a fork in a restaurant. Diego was that. We are speechless in their moments of genius and, in their moments of weakness, we laugh low, without showing much, but as if we were to say, “Oh, this Diego”. He's someone we love.
SL: A question about Latin America. How do you see the continent today? What are your main challenges?
RB: It is very difficult to talk about the continent because this is one of the most fragmented periods since the 1980s. There is no continuity with progressive governments. Without union, it is not possible to reach the mass necessary to face certain issues in multilateral organizations with a unique position. So it seems to me that every country has its own agenda.
Where do you put Peru, a country that has been going through a huge institutional crisis for ten years? Where do you place Colombia, which after many years of right-wing hegemony is now led by a left-wing government? Social processes take longer than government periods.
We are seeking providential leadership, while the collective seems to me to be the only way to find a solution to the problems of the continent. Therefore, it is very difficult to speak of a Latin America.
SL: How do you see the strengthening of the BRICS and the emergence of a new multipolar world?
RB: First of all, I would like to emphasize that the existence of BRICS is positive. But I will criticize some aspects. What's the matter? The history of the BRICS showed that weak institutionalization is an obstacle when the winds are against us. The BRICS enjoyed an expansion period that coincided with the increased value of raw materials. After the 2008 crisis, there was a calming and the BRICS were almost no longer mentioned.
Today, they are coming back thanks to Lula's energy, which is like a fish in the water in this universe. Brazil is lucky to have Lula, because Lula is like a Brazilian Mandela. So he's a wonderful prow figure. Since Brazil has excellent foreign policy executives, this seems to work. But it is always necessary to observe the institutional structure. If it's solid, the BRICS have a chance to stay. On the other hand, if it's weak, they'll be vulnerable. At the moment, the BRICS are more a good idea than a powerful tool.
Multipolarity is the immediate trend, but it is not the definitive trend. I'd like it to be. The United States will continue to be important and leaders in certain areas for a few years.
SL: Could you talk a little about China?
RB: China's advance is more solid than the United States retreat. No one seems to have noticed that China, which sells everything one can imagine, does not export ideology, unlike the left of the 1960s and 1970s, which exported revolution. China is exporting its money, its projects, its ability to carry out works with the best possible funding, at rates that challenge the entire competition. From my point of view, that's a better formula. China is smart enough not to enter a war.
SL: Last question, you are a great knower of France. What does France mean to you?
RB: If football is the second nature, France is the third nature, because my mother and my maternal grandmother spoke piemontese, which is a language close to French. I started studying French when I was very young. From Monsieur et Madame Vincent a La condition humaine, nothing of what is spoken in French is strange to me. It is an extraordinarily important country because once again it is difficult for me to find episodes in my life with which I do not associate a book in French or the French language. France is part of my life.
en_subtitle: “I am peronist and time, far from tempering my ideological convictions, has strengthened my loyalty to my ideas”

title: PF entrega a Alexandre de Moraes em junho relatório final do caso das joias ilegais de Bolsonaro
subtitle: Com a investigação das joias concluída, a Polícia Federal quer avançar no inquérito do golpe, que pode levar à prisão de Bolsonaro
content:
O relatório final da Polícia Federal sobre a investigação acerca das joias ilegais recebidas por Jair Bolsonaro deverá ser entregue ao Supremo Tribunal Federal (STF) em junho, informa Bela Megale,do jornal 
Entre 25 de abril e 11 de maio, a PF realizou as últimas diligências, que incluíram uma viagem aos Estados Unidos para entrevistar testemunhas e visitar lojas onde as joias foram comercializadas ilegalmente. Essas diligências resultaram no acesso a imagens inéditas e na coleta de depoimentos que detalham a venda e recompra ilícitas do "kit ouro branco".
O conjunto de joias foi presenteado a Bolsonaro durante uma visita oficial à Arábia Saudita em 2019. O valor total dos itens é estimado em pelo menos R$ 500 mil. O Tribunal de Contas da União (TCU) já determinou que esses presentes pertencem ao acervo da Presidência da República e não a Bolsonaro.
Com o avanço na investigação das joias, a PF se prepara para concluir outro inquérito de grande relevância: a investigação sobre a tentativa de golpe que visava manter Bolsonaro no poder após sua derrota para o presidente Lula (PT) nas eleições de 2022. Um parecer recente da Procuradoria-Geral da República (PGR) afirmou que essa investigação está "em via de conclusão" e é considerada a que tem maior potencial de resultar na prisão de Bolsonaro.
en_title: PF delivers to Alexandre de Moraes in June final report on the case of Bolsonaro's illegal jewelry
en_content:
The final report of the Federal Police on the investigation of illegal jewelry received by Jair Bolsonaro should be delivered to the Supreme Court (STF) in June, says Bela Megale, from the newspaper
Between 25 April and 11 May, the PF conducted the last stages, which included a trip to the United States to interview witnesses and visit shops where the jewels were illegally marketed. These diligences resulted in access to unpublished images and the collection of testimonies detailing the illicit sale and rebuy of the "white gold kit".
The jewelry set was presented to Bolsonaro during an official visit to Saudi Arabia in 2019. The total value of the items is estimated at at least R $ 500 thousand. The Court of Auditors of the Union (TCU) has already determined that these gifts belong to the acquis of the Presidency of the Republic and not to Bolsonaro.
With the advance in jewelry research, the PF prepares to complete another investigation of great relevance: the investigation into the coup attempt aimed at keeping Bolsonaro in power after his defeat to President Lula (PT) in the elections of 2022. A recent opinion by the Attorney General of the Republic (PGR) stated that this investigation is "in the way of conclusion" and is considered to have the greatest potential to result in Bolsonaro's arrest.
en_subtitle: With the investigation of the jewels completed, the Federal Police want to advance the investigation of the coup, which can lead to the arrest of Bolsonaro

title: Moraes tira sarro de frase de Eduardo Bolsonaro: 'cabo e soldado estão presos e o STF continua aberto' (vídeo)
subtitle: Ironia faz alusão aos atos golpistas do 8/1 e à declaração feita pelo parlamentar em 2018, de que bastariam apenas "um cabo e um soldado" para fechar o STF
content:
O ministro do Supremo Tribunal Federal (STF) Alexandre de Moraes ironizou uma fala antiga do deputado federal Eduardo Bolsonaro (PL-SP) em que ele afirmava que bastariam apenas um “cabo e um soldado” para fechar a Suprema Corte. “Todos se recordam que bastava um cabo e um soldado para fechar o Supremo Tribunal Federal. O cabo, o soldado, o coronel, estão todos presos. E o Supremo Tribunal Federal aberto e funcionando. Mas se disse que bastaria um cabo e um soldado”, disse Moraes sem se referir diretamente ao parlamentar, durante um seminário do Tribunal Superior Eleitoral (TSE), realizado nesta quarta-feira (22), de acordo com o jornal 
A frase de Eduardo Bolsonaro foi proferida durante a campanha eleitoral de 2018. Na ocasião, o deputado federal declarou, em um curso para aspirantes a concursos públicos, que "se quiser fechar o STF, você não manda nem um jipe. Manda um soldado e um cabo". 
Para o ministro, o confronto com o Judiciário foi uma das estratégias do "novo populismo" que culminou nos atos golpistas do dia 8 de janeiro do ano passado. “Como não foi um cabo e um soldado, foram milhares de pessoas que destruíram o prédio do Supremo Tribunal Federal. Se foi para o confronto ao Judiciário, para tentar, exatamente, garantir esse novo populismo”, disse Moraes.
Assista:
en_title: Moraes takes phrase sarro from Eduardo Bolsonaro: 'cabo and soldier are stuck and the STF remains open' (video)
en_content:
The Minister of the Supreme Court (STF) Alexandre de Moraes ironized an old speech by Congressman Eduardo Bolsonaro (PL-SP) in which he claimed that it would only be enough a "cable and a soldier" to close the Supreme Court. “Everyone remembers that a cable and a soldier was enough to close the Supreme Court. The cable, the soldier, the colonel, they're all under arrest. And the Supreme Court open and functioning. But if he said that a cable and a soldier would be enough,” Moraes said without referring directly to the parliamentarian, during a seminar of the Superior Electoral Court (TSE), held on Wednesday (22), according to the newspaper
Eduardo Bolsonaro's sentence was pronounced during the 2018 election campaign. On the occasion, the federal deputy declared, in a course for aspiring public tenders, that "if you want to close the STF, you do not send a jeep. Send a soldier and a cable."
For the minister, the confrontation with the judiciary was one of the strategies of the "new populism" that culminated in the coup acts of January 8 last year. “As it was not a cable and a soldier, it was thousands of people who destroyed the Supreme Court building. If he went to the judiciary confrontation, to try, exactly, to guarantee this new populism,” Moraes said.
